title: Dependent fields vs select vs inline validation vs complete complex form
summary: Use dependent fields when one completed controlling answer determines which later field values are valid, available, required, cleared, or stale.
decisionRules: Prefer dependent fields when a controlling field narrows a dependent field's options, requirement, value, availability, validation, or stale state on the same form record.
Prefer select when the user only needs to choose one value from a finite list and no other field changes that list after selection.
Prefer inline validation when one field's own value is wrong and can be corrected locally without recomputing another field's available choices.
Prefer complete complex form when dependency rules span several sections and users need section status, cross-section validation, review, and error-summary navigation.
Dependent fields must show which upstream answer controls the downstream field and must update helper text, options, required status, and error state when that answer changes.
When the controlling answer changes, clear or mark stale any dependent value that is no longer valid; do not silently keep an incompatible downstream answer.
Keep dependent fields in logical reading order after their controlling field, unless the product gives a clear summary of the dependency and recovery path.
Avoid a disabled dependent control with no explanation; if it cannot be used yet, explain the prerequisite and keep it discoverable to keyboard and assistive technology users.

Use conditional reveal when the downstream question should appear only after a related answer, but use dependent fields when the downstream control remains part of the form and its options or validity depend on another value.
Do not model independent choices as dependent just to reduce visible options; users should still be able to compare independent answers when comparison matters.
Server-side validation must enforce the same dependency rules as the UI because stale or incompatible dependent values can arrive through reloads, APIs, or browser manipulation.

If dependency rules are many-to-many, nested, asynchronous, permission-based, or opaque, show loading, unavailable, conflict, and fallback states rather than pretending a simple parent-child select is enough.
failureModes: A subcategory remains selected after category changes, so the submitted record contains an impossible pair.
The dependent field is disabled without saying which controlling field must be answered first.
Options update visually but the error text still describes the old controlling answer.
A many-to-many dependency is forced into a one-parent select, hiding valid options.
A dependent field appears before its controlling field in the reading order, so keyboard and screen reader users encounter a blocked field first.
Client-side dependency filtering is bypassed and the backend accepts an incompatible controlled value.

sources.note: ServiceNow documents choice and reference fields whose available values depend on another same-table field, including category-to-subcategory behavior and many-to-many caveats.
Salesforce documents dependent picklists filtering one picklist based on a controlling value to guide users, save UI space, and improve data integrity.
USWDS form guidance anchors disabled-state and validation-accessibility requirements for dependent controls that are unavailable until prerequisites are met.
GOV.UK radio guidance provides the boundary for simple conditional reveal, which is different from option filtering and stale dependent-value management.
